data.title: Trending stocks as Wall Street ends week lower amid geopolitical volatility
data.description:
Wall Street closed the trading week lower, though equities rebounded from intra-week lows following a volatile stretch marked by heightened geopolitical uncertainty.

Geopolitical tensions continued as U.S. President Donald Trump reiterated his interest in Greenland during remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos. Trump said he plans to begin negotiations “immediately” to seek control of the territory, adding to global uncertainty that pressured investor sentiment.

Gold hit record highs, and the dollar tumbled against a spiking yen, signaling safe-haven demand.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) lost -0.4%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped -0.1%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -0.5%.

Here’s what caught investor attention this week:

Intel (INTC) shares fell after issuing guidance for the coming quarter that was below Wall Street's forecast. The chipmaker expects Q1 revenue to be between $11.7B and $12.7B, with the midpoint of $12.2B below the $12.56B analysts were anticipating. Amid the ongoing server CPU shortage, CFO David Zinsner said the company's “available supply” will be at its lowest level in the quarter before rebounding in the second quarter and beyond.

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) was in focus after U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday filed a $5B lawsuit against the bank and its longtime CEO Jamie Dimon, alleging that the bank cut ties with him and his businesses for political reasons, according to a media report. The suit alleges JPMorgan (JPM) committed trade libel and breached its implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, Fox Business reported, citing the complaint, which was filed in Miami-Dade County state court. Trump also alleged that Dimon ran afoul of Florida's Unfair and Deceptive Trade Practices Act.

Celestica (CLS) shares sank on Thursday after Digitimes reported that Google's  tensor processing unit servers may be assembled by another supplier. Google has expanded assembly orders for its L10-L11 TPU servers to Inventec and other Taiwanese manufacturers, the news outlet added, citing sources. The servers beyond the L10 were previously assembled by Celestica.

Microsoft (MSFT) was in focus after Elon Musk sought damages of $79B-$134B from OpenAI (OPENAI) and Microsoft (MSFT) over allegations that the ChatGPT maker defrauded him when it abandoned its founding nonprofit roots last year. Musk’s lawsuit claims that OpenAI, which was originally set up as a nonprofit with his early contributions, shifted away from that mission and became a for-profit entity through its partnership with Microsoft, and that he was therefore defrauded of the financial gains he believes he should have received.

Sandisk (SNDK) shares continued its upward trajectory, adding over nearly 12% in the week. The stock has surged 112% so far this year, outperforming the S&P 500 Index’s gain of about 1% and ranking the stock among the index’s top performers in 2026. The rally reflects a shift in the artificial intelligence trade toward storage stocks, along with global flash memory shortages that have allowed the company to raise prices, according to S3.

Netflix (NFLX) made a revised all-cash offer for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) at $27.75 per share. Warner Bros. (WBD) said it will hold a special meeting of shareholders to vote on the deal before April. Meanwhile, rival bidder Paramount Skydance (PSKY) on Thursday extended the deadline for its hostile tender offer to February 20, shortly after Netflix revised its bid to an all-cash offer to speed up closing and provide greater certainty to investors.

The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC) fell after disclosing in a filing that its largest shareholder Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) may unload a 27.5% stake in the company.

Micron Technology (MU) gained after announcing plans last week to acquire a chip fabrication site in Taiwan from Powerchip Semiconductor Manufacturing in an all-cash deal worth $1.8B. The acquisition is expected to close during the second half of 2026. It is projected to contribute to meaningful DRAM (dynamic random access memory) wafer output by the second half of calendar year 2027.

Alibaba (BABA) was in focus after Bloomberg News reported that the Chinese e-commerce giant is preparing for an IPO of its chipmaking unit T-Head. The company plans to restructure the unit as a business partly owned by employees. It will then explore an IPO, though the timing for that remains unclear, the report added.

Capital One Financial (COF) agreed to acquire Brex, a fintech that specializes in technology for credit cards, expenses, and rewards, for $5.15B. Brex was founded in 2017 to serve other startups that were finding it challenging to get corporate cards from traditional companies.

data.title: Real estate stocks decline amid geopolitical developments, weak earnings
data.description:
Real estate stocks were largely in red this week, with the sector kicking off the quarterly earnings season on a sour note and heightened geopolitical uncertainty leading to increased volatility.

Geopolitical developments took center stage after President Donald Trump reiterated his interest in Greenland during remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. Trump said he intends to begin negotiations “immediately” to pursue control of the territory, adding to broader global uncertainty that weighed on investor sentiment.

Meanwhile, the first batch of real estate earnings failed to impress investors.

Among largecap stocks, Prologis (PLD) led the weekly losers. The stock lost 4.22% during the course of the week to close at $127.15.

The logistics real estate company reported Q4 attributable core FFO per share of $1.44, down from $1.50 in the same period of 2024. The metric was impacted by a net promote expense.

Crown Castle (CCI) (down 4.21% week-over-week to $86.60) was the second biggest loser. The week saw KeyBanc Capital Markets and Goldman Sachs lower their price target on CCI.

Extra Space Storage (EXR) (-3.81% W/W to $141.52) was the third on the list.

For the winners, Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) (+4.24% W/W to $59.69), KE Holdings (BEKE) (+3.82% to $18.22), and CoStar Group (CSGP) (+3.50% W/W to $65.60) led the pack.

KE Holdings had detailed share buybacks in its latest 6-K filing.

Among the midcap stocks, Kilroy Realty (KRC) (-6.88% W/W to $35.59), SL Green Realty (SLG) (-5.59% W/W to $45.90), and Colliers International Group (CIGI) (-5.18% W/W to $139.85) led the weekly losers.

Compass (COMP) (+5.88% W/W to $13.33) and Rithm Capital (RITM) (+3.79% W/W to $11.78) were significant gainers.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Energy markets heat up amid frigid temperatures
data.description:
Natural gas prices have soared this week, with front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) rising more than 70% to the highest level in more than five years as a major winter storm is set to hit large swaths of the U.S. over the coming days.  

Nearly 140M people were under a winter storm warning while the National Weather Service warned of a widespread heavy snowfall and catastrophic ice storm stretching from east Texas to North Carolina over the weekend.

Contracts for near-term deliveries were the biggest gainers in natural gas prices, The Wall Street Journal reported, adding that futures for February delivery of the heating and power-generation fuel have recorded the biggest three-day percentage gain through Thursday.

Futures (NG1:COM) extended the rally on Friday, adding ~6% to settle at $5.35 compared to $3.103 at the end of the previous week.

Natural gas producers Comstock Resources (CRK), Expand Energy (EXE), Range Resources (RRC), Coterra Energy (CTRA), Antero Resources (AR), and EQT (EQT) closed the week higher. ETFs United States Natural Gas Fund (UNG) and ProShares Ultra Bloomberg Natural Gas ETF (BOIL) surged, adding ~36% and ~75% for the week, respectively.

Further fueling the rally were hedge funds that had turned more bearish on gas based on projections of milder winter conditions last week. The shift in forecasts forced them to buy futures to cover those short bets this week, further fueling the rally.  Soaring gas prices are expected to impact nearly 61M American homes that use natural gas for heating and 57M homes that use electricity, much of which is produced by burning gas.

According to longer-term forecasts, freezing temperatures are expected to last well into February. Matt Rogers, a meteorologist and president of commercial forecaster Commodity Weather Group, said that the two weeks ending Feb. 4 are expected to be the coldest since 1985 based on heating-degree days—a metric traders use to measure demand.

data.title: Insider Trades: GameStop, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley among notable names this week
data.description:
Costco (COST) insiders reported notable stock sales in January, with Executive Vice President Teresa A. Jones selling 850 shares on January 21 at an average price of $986.26, generating proceeds of about $838K, while still holding over 4,200 shares. Director Susan L. Decker also trimmed her stake, selling 458 shares on January 16 at roughly $955 per share for proceeds of $437K, though she continues to retain a sizeable position of more than 8,500 shares.

Goldman Sachs (GS) saw heavy insider selling in mid-January, as Director David A. Viniar executed multiple transactions on January 16, unloading shares across three sales at prices ranging from about $964 to $971. In total, Viniar sold roughly 68,000 shares for more than $65M, though he continues to hold a substantial stake in the firm, with post-transaction holdings exceeding 750,000 shares.

Morgan Stanley (MS) insiders executed a wave of coordinated selling on January 20, with multiple senior executives—including the CFO, Co-President, and Chief Risk Officer—reducing their holdings at prices clustered around the mid-$180s. In aggregate, more than 104,000 shares were sold for roughly $19M, while all executives involved continue to hold meaningful stakes in the firm.

Argan (AGX) insiders reported sizeable stock sales in mid-January, with Director Cynthia Flanders selling 19,000 shares on January 21 at an average price of $386.70, totaling about $7.3M in proceeds. Director William F. Leimkuhler also reduced his position, selling just over 11,000 shares on January 16 at roughly $379 per share for about $4.2M, while both directors continue to hold meaningful stakes in the company.

GameStop (GME) disclosed aggressive insider buying this week, as President, CEO, and Chairman Ryan Cohen added 1 million shares across two purchases on January 20–21 at prices around $21 per share. The buys totaled roughly $21.4 million, lifting Cohen’s stake to more than 38.3 million shares and underscoring a strong vote of confidence from the company’s top executive.

Under Armour (UAA) saw substantial insider accumulation last week, as 10% owner Prem Watsa and related entities purchased shares across three consecutive sessions at prices between $5.60 and $5.89. In total, nearly 8.6 million shares were acquired for roughly $49.7 million, boosting the group’s ownership to just over 18 million shares and signaling strong conviction at current price levels.

Ally Financial (ALLY) disclosed insider buying late in the week, with CEO Michael George Rhodes purchasing 23,800 shares on January 23 at an average price of $41.68. The transaction totaled just under $1M and increased Rhodes’ holdings to 49,434 shares.

Mission Produce (AVO) attracted sustained insider buying over three consecutive sessions, as 10% owner Globalharvest Holdings Venture steadily added to its stake between January 15 and January 20 at prices ranging from $11.76 to $12.50. In total, more than 1.6 million shares were purchased for roughly $19.4M, lifting Globalharvest’s ownership to about 9.46M shares.

data.title: Trump warns Canada with 100% tariffs over China trade deal
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Saturday threatened to implement 100% tariffs on goods imported from Canada if his country’s northern neighbor moves forward with a newly signed trade agreement with China.

"If Canada makes a deal with China, it will immediately be hit with a 100% tariff against all Canadian goods and products coming into the U.S.A.," Trump wrote in a social media post.

His latest retaliatory tactic came after Canada struck a trade deal with China last week to reduce levies on Chinese electric vehicles in exchange for tariff concessions on canola and other Canadian goods.

This week, the Republican president said at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, that “Canada lives because of the United States,” drawing a strong rebuke from Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney.

"If Governor Carney thinks he is going to make Canada a 'drop-off port' for China to send goods and products into the United States, he is sorely mistaken," Trump wrote. "China will eat Canada alive, completely devour it, including the destruction of their businesses, social fabric, and general way of life."

Canada-focused ETFs: (EWC), (FXC), (FLCA), and (BBCA).

ETFs tracking the S&P 500 (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

data.title: Lemonade, XP jump, Old Republic, XXI fall: week's financials wrap
data.description:
Financial stocks endured another turbulent week as whipsawing trade and geopolitical headlines, alongside corporate earnings and fresh economic data, kept investors busy, even with a holiday at the start of the week.

Lemonade (LMND) rose the most of any financial stock (with a market cap over $2B) in the five trading sessions ended Jan. 23, 2026, surging 16% after unveiling its insurance for self-driving cars, starting with Tesla FSD (TSLA);

Brazilian investment bank XP (XP) also gained 16%;

Canada-based asset manager Sprott (SII) gapped up 15%;

WisdomTree (WT) advanced 15% as well; and

Inter & Co (INTR) rose 14%.

On the losers, Old Republic International (ORI) tumbled 12% after the insurance firm turned in worse-than-expected Q4 2025 earnings as specialty insurance underwriting results underwhelmed;

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) treasury firm Twenty One Capital (XXI) slid 11%;

Rocket Companies (RKT) dropped 10%;

HCI Group (HCI) retreated 9.4%; and

Coinbase Global (COIN), the U.S.'s largest crypto exchange, fell 8.4%.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Amgen, Chevron and Procter & Gamble among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday, after the week saw the start of another earnings season and news of a framework deal as Trump backed off tariff threats against Europe.

For the week, Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and Dow (DJI) rose over 2% and 1.8%, respectively.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Bernstein downgrades Amgen, says 2026 could be ‘waiting year’

Bernstein has downgraded Amgen (AMGN) to Market Perform from Outperform, arguing that 2026 could be a “waiting year” for the pharma giant and its stock will remain within 15% of its $335 per share target for the rest of the year.

While Amgen’s obesity candidate MariTide can generate above-consensus expectations, analyst Courtney Breen argued that any upside revisions to the once-monthly drug are unlikely until its first Phase 3 trial generates data, potentially in Q1 2027.

Breen also cited competitive pressure on Amgen’s cholesterol drug, Repatha, from Merck’s (MRK) oral PCSK9 inhibitor, Enlicitide, which is expected to launch in H2.

JPM upgrades Chevron, Suncor; ConocoPhillips, Cenovus receives cut

Chevron (CVX) was upgraded to Overweight from Neutral with a $176 PT at J.P. Morgan, based on the view that the company is in an attractive phase of its investment cycle following the completion of the Hess merger, while structural cost reduction efforts are on track to deliver $3B-$4B in annual run-rate savings by 2026.

Analyst Arun Jayaram moved ConocoPhillips (COP) to Neutral from Overweight with a $98 PT, saying the stock trades at premium FCF/EV yields to Big Oil peers in 2026-27. Meanwhile, he upgraded Suncor Energy (SU) to Overweight from Neutral with a C$75 PT, citing "remarkable" execution progress under the current management team.

The brokerage also lowered Cenovus Energy (CVE) to Neutral from Overweight with a C425 price target on the stock, saying the company has hit its stride from an execution standpoint both upstream and downstream.

JPMorgan gives bullish rating to Procter & Gamble

Procter & Gamble (PG) was upgraded by JP Morgan to Overweight from Neutral and assigned a price target of $165 on the stock.

Analyst Andrea Teixeira believes the company is poised to accelerate organic sales growth, improve margin rates in the medium term, and see re-rating back to historical valuation multiples.

"We believe there is upside to consensus longer term as categories are recovering growth in the U.S. We believe PG will regain market share given its strong brand equity, marketing capabilities, and supply chain excellence/resilience positively in the current operating environment," wrote Texeira.

Netflix in focus after quarterly report

Analysts, in general, found Netflix’s (NFLX) quarterly results as somewhat “mixed” and “underwhelming”. However, they said that the streaming giant is poised for long-term growth and that the current weakness 'a buying opportunity' for investors.

Even though Wedbush and Jefferies made no change in their ratings and PT, Needham, Morgan Stanley and Canaccord Genuity cut price target for the stock.

Needham maintained Buy rating and views the strong content slate the platform has for 2026, its focus on international growth and diversification into complementary businesses as reasons for the bullish rating. The brokerage lowered PT by $30 to $120.

Similarly, Morgan Stanley continued to stay bullish for its ability to drive adj. EPS growth over 20% annually through 2028E as it continues to scale the world's largest streaming business. It cut PT by $10 to $110. Canaccord Genuity cut PT by $27.50 to $125.

Deutsche Bank joins bull camp on Applied Materials

Deutsche Bank became the latest Wall Street firm to upgrade Applied Materials (AMAT) as it hiked the semiconductor equipment maker to Buy from Neutral and put a $390 price target on the stock.

Analyst Melissa Weathers said the upgrade reflects a “much more constructive [wafer fab equipment] environment entering 2026 and 2027.” She also noted that memory sellers are “accelerating” the construction and expansion of new DRAM fabs, which should drive DRAM wafer fab equipment spending higher in the medium term.

Susquehanna also upgraded British semiconductor firm Arm (ARM) to Positive from Neutral and upped its PT to $150, as it has “increased confidence” it can scale its data center and networking revenue.

Jefferies upgraded Ramaco Resources (METC) to Buy from Hold with a $30 price target, saying the risk to consensus met coal price forecasts is clearly to the upside while ongoing and escalating geopolitical risks related to critical minerals are positive for the stock.

Stifel upgraded Datadog (DDOG), citing an “attractive” risk-reward profile. Analyst Brad Reback, who raised his rating to Buy from Hold, but cut his price target to $160 from $205, added that the company is seeing an acceleration in its core business, citing recent commentary from Datadog management at an investor conference.

data.title: 24 of 31 companies post EPS growth in latest earnings week: Earnings scorecard
data.description:
Earnings this week delivered a broad read on the U.S. economy, with results spanning semiconductors, industrials, energy, health care, and a heavy concentration of financials. Banks and capital markets firms led the slate, offering fresh clues on credit trends and rate sensitivity, while industrial and technology names reflected shifting demand and cost dynamics across the economy.

Earnings Roundup:

Of the 31 S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, 24 beat EPS expectations, 3 missed and 4 came in line. On a year-over-year basis, 24 companies posted higher earnings.

On the top line, 24 companies topped revenue estimates, while 7 fell short. Year over year, 29 companies reported revenue growth.

 

 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week:

Intel (INTC) shares fell XXX% on Friday after the semiconductor giant reported stronger-than-expected fourth-quarter results, but issued guidance for the coming quarter that was below Wall Street's forecast.

GE Aerospace (GE) fell 7% Thursday despite reporting Q4 adjusted earnings and FY 2026 guidance that topped Wall Street expectations, as analysts noted the company's torrid growth may be slowing a bit.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) reported better-than-expected revenue for Q4 2025 on Tuesday thanks to its Pharma and MedTech divisions. However, the shares of the New Brunswick, New Jersey-based healthcare giant ended almost flat as its adjusted earnings for the quarter did not exceed the consensus.

Netflix stock (NFLX) slipped 2% after posting fourth-quarter results that narrowly beat expectations. The streaming pioneer narrowly topped Street consensus for revenues with $12.05B (reflecting year-over-year growth of 17.6%), while net income rose 29% year-over-year to $2.42B and earnings per share edged Wall Street forecasts.

Procter & Gamble (PG) closed 2.6% higher on Thursday after the household products giant missed FQ2 revenue estimates. The Cincinnati-based company delivered a modest EPS beat with steady YoY earnings.

Shares of 3M (MMM) fell to a three-month low Tuesday after the industrial conglomerate issued a 2026 earnings forecast that narrowly missed Wall Street expectations, highlighting the difficulty of sustaining its turnaround amid uneven economic conditions. For the fourth quarter, 3M (MMM) reported adjusted earnings of $1.83 a share, edging past the $1.80 analysts had projected.

Kinder Morgan (KMI) reported an EPS beat alongside solid YoY growth in both earnings and revenue.

Earnings next week

Industrials are set to lead next week’s earnings calendar, with the highest number of companies reporting, highlighted by Boeing (BA), Caterpillar (CAT), Lockheed Martin (LMT), and RTX (RTX). Other active sectors include Financials—American Express (AXP), Mastercard (MA), and Blackstone (BX); Information Technology—Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and International Business Machines (IBM); Health Care—UnitedHealth (UNH) and Thermo Fisher (TMO); Communication Services—with results from Meta Platforms (META), AT&T (T), and Verizon (VZ); and Consumer Discretionary, featuring heavyweights such as Tesla (TSLA) and Starbucks (SBUX).

data.title: Memory is the hottest part of the market. Can the rally continue?
data.description:
Spurred by insatiable demand from data centers and all things related to artificial intelligence, memory, and storage makers have become the hottest part of the stock market. Companies like Micron (MU), Sandisk (SNDK) and Seagate Technology (STX) have seen their valuations soar, as investors flock to these companies.

And yet, with valuations rising rapidly, some have wondered whether the party can continue. Wedbush Securities believes it can. 

Analyst Matt Bryson, who covers Sandisk, Seagate, and Western Digital (WDC) (which spun off Sandisk in February 2025), believes the shortfall for hard-disk drives and memory has expanded, compared to what's being advertised by the cloud service providers. Industry sources told Bryson that shipments are likely to fall short of requirements by at least 200, if not 300 (or more) exabytes. An exabyte is 1B gigabytes.

“And, while we believe any calculations involve double ordering by customers, we also think industry supply growth is likely capped at ~20% the next few years, while historically data growth has been closer to ~30% implying the EB supply gap will only grow for some time to come,” Bryson explained. “Finally, while we expect industry EPS gains will be capped by existing [long-term agreements] that set large customer pricing through at least C1H'26, we also believe vendors likely benefit from a step function in PX when they transition to the next set of [long-term agreements] (likely C2H'26 into CY'27), a shift that isn't yet contemplated by Street models.”

Bryson raised his price target on Sandisk to $600 from $260 and also increased his targets on Seagate and Western Digital to $380 and $260 from $290 and $200, respectively.

Shares of all three have soared in recent months, with Sandisk leading the way, up nearly 1,300% over the past year. Western Digital's near 370% gain is nothing to sneeze at, nor is the roughly 220% gain for Seagate.

Micron, which trades in concert with the trio, has seen its shares rise nearly 280%, amid strong demand for its high-bandwidth memory products. South Korea's Sk hynix  (HXSC.F) and Samsung (SSNLF), both of which are partners with OpenAI (OPENAI) on its $500B Stargate project, have also benefited.

Sk hynix recently said it is considering listing its shares in the U.S. amid the AI boom. SK hynix, which supplies memory to Nvidia (NVDA), has been working hard to boost its supply of high-bandwidth memory for Nvidia and others to meet rising demand.

NAND boom

There are two main areas of the memory market: dynamic random access memory, or DRAM, and NAND. DRAM is often used for memory tasks that are active, while NAND is used for tasks that are slower and more long-term. NAND is primarily used in USB drives, phones, and solid-state drives.

And while both parts of the market have seen prices soar, NAND may be poised for more upside, Bryson added.

“We see greater near-term upside in NAND as our conversations/checks suggest a rapid lift in secondary market [spot pricing in November and December] with contract [spot pricing] set to lift meaningfully in March (50%+),” Bryson added. “Finally, while we believe these two factors are encapsulated in buy-side models, early conversations indicate June pricing gains will also be well into the double digits (due to the magnitude of the continued shortfall in supply vs. demand), a result that would push industry margins well above historical peaks and which we don't believe is yet baked into expectations.”

Seagate is set to report its next fiscal quarterly results after the close of trading on Jan. 27, while Western Digital and Sandisk both report on Jan. 29.

data.title: Novo sued for working with Teva to delay generic Victoza
data.description:
A class action lawsuit was filed in New York federal court on Friday accusing Novo Nordisk (NVO) of running a "pay-for-delay" scheme with Teva Pharmaceutical (TEVA) to unlawfully delay the market entry of cheaper generic versions of the Danish drugmaker’s diabetes drug Victoza.

South Carolina-based drug wholesaler Smith Drug filed the suit in the U.S. District Court for the Eastern District of New York, alleging that if not for the Novo (NVO)–Teva (TEVA) deal, generics against Victoza would have been available in the market since 2023.

In 2024, Teva (TEVA) launched the first generic version of Victoza, Novo's (NVO) first-generation GLP-1, which had amassed $1.7B in annual sales as of April of that year.

While the lawsuit didn’t name the Israel-based drugmaker as a defendant, it alleged that Novo illegally paid to delay Teva's (TEVA) generic under a 2019 settlement that resolved a patent dispute between the two companies.

According to Reuters, the suit seeks an unspecified amount of monetary damages for a proposed class of Victoza buyers.
